metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `test_judge_precision_recall` runs the actual LLM prompt through real inference (required for a genuine quality gate) or uses FakeRunner (which makes the precision/recall assertion tautological); if the intent is a real-inference quality gate, move it to `tests/external/` and mark it accordingly (e.g. `@pytest.mark.external` or `skipif no API key`), and explicitly state this in the Testing section.
findings.finding: The `test_judge_precision_recall` fixture test is placed in `tests/unit/` alongside FakeRunner-based tests, but a precision/recall measurement over a labeled pair set requires actual LLM inference through the real prompt path — not an offline FakeRunner. A FakeRunner returns whatever the test author programs it to return, making the precision≥0.8 / recall≥0.6 assertion trivially self-fulfilling (the fake is configured to match the fixture labels by construction). The plan says this test is 'NOT the prompt-eval harness' but never clarifies whether it uses real inference or the fake; the unit/ location strongly implies the fake.
findings.location: Testing section — `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py::test_judge_precision_recall`
findings.suggested_fix: Move `test_judge_precision_recall` to `tests/external/` (or a dedicated `tests/quality/` tier), wire it through the real runner path (not FakeRunner), gate it with `pytest.mark.skipif(not os.environ.get('ANTHROPIC_API_KEY'), reason='needs live LLM')`, and update the proving command accordingly. The other unit tests (test_agree, test_disagree_downgrade, etc.) correctly use FakeRunner to test aggregation/surfacing logic — only the precision/recall test needs real inference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a corresponding lightweight test or grep-based check) that verifies `overlap_judge.md` contains each required structural element — e.g., a test that loads the prompt and asserts the presence of the artifact-citation requirement, abstain instruction, and both-orderings instruction.
findings.finding: The rubric prompt file (`overlap_judge.md`) has four required must-encode elements (4-way classification, shared-artifact gate, both-orderings instruction, abstain-when-unsure criterion) but no AC or proving command verifies that the shipped prompt file actually contains them — an executor can ship a stub prompt and all unit tests still pass.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt … MUST encode: the 4-way classification, the require-a-cited-shared-artifact gate, the both-orderings instruction, and the abstain-when-unsure criterion'; Acceptance Criteria — no AC covers prompt content.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `test_judge_precision_recall` invokes the real LLM (or a faithful recorded-replay) against the 12-pair fixture — if it uses FakeRunner, add a note on how ground-truth verdicts are sourced without making the test tautological, or move the live prompt evaluation to a named integration/eval target.
findings.finding: `test_judge_precision_recall` may be a tautology (self-authored oracle) if it runs through the same FakeRunner used by the other unit tests: you would configure the fake to return whatever verdicts produce the desired precision/recall, making the assertion vacuously true and locking in no real quality signal about the LLM prompt.
findings.location: Testing section — `test_judge_precision_recall`, `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Testing section: '`test_judge_precision_recall` uses a recorded-replay runner (VCR/cassette) or a live LLM call behind a pytest mark (`@pytest.mark.llm_eval`) so that the judge prompt is genuinely exercised against the labeled pairs. FakeRunner is NOT used for this test.' This preserves offline CI for unit tests while ensuring the precision/recall gate has real signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `test_judge_precision_recall` runs against a real LLM (integration) or FakeRunner (unit); if FakeRunner, add a note stating that live-path validation is explicitly deferred to S7 integration testing, and reference the S7 story.
findings.finding: `test_judge_precision_recall` is placed in `tests/unit/` alongside FakeRunner tests; the plan does not state whether this test uses a real LLM or a pre-scripted FakeRunner. If FakeRunner, the fixture labels are effectively baked into the fake responses, making precision/recall trivially achievable and leaving the live LLM path entirely unexercised by any AC in this story.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Precision/recall check as a PYTEST FIXTURE test (NOT the prompt-eval harness … FakeRunner)'; Acceptance Criteria — last bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/config.py` → `LLMConfig.from_env` body as an explicit edit target in the Scope section, specifying that `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD", "overlap_conf_threshold", 0.7)` (or float variant) and the surface_cap equivalent must be wired there.
findings.finding: LLMConfig.from_env is an unenumerated edit target. Adding overlap_conf_threshold and overlap_surface_cap fields to LLMConfig requires adding their resolution logic inside from_env (via _llm_int calls for REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD / REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP env vars and the corresponding file-table keys). The Scope section lists config keys on LLMConfig but does not name from_env as a touched location, leaving an implementor uncertain whether from_env must be updated.
findings.location: ## Config (LLMConfig, not VerifyConfig) / ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add: 'Edit LLMConfig.from_env in src/rebar/llm/config.py to resolve overlap_conf_threshold via _llm_float (or _llm_int) from REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD / file key, and overlap_surface_cap from REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP / file key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Config section: the exact `LLMConfig` field types (`float` for `overlap_conf_threshold`, `int` for `overlap_surface_cap`), the exact env var names (`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD`, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP`), the exact `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys, and that `from_env()` must add a `_llm_float` helper (or reuse `_llm_str` + parse) since `_llm_int` cannot represent 0.7.
findings.finding: `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` are described as fields on `LLMConfig` with `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys, but `src/rebar/llm/config.py` contains neither field nor any `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` resolution logic in `LLMConfig.from_env()`. The plan specifies the field names and defaults but not their resolution wiring (which `_llm_int` call, which file_key string, which env var name maps to which field). This is a referenced-but-unspecified config extension.
findings.location: Config section: 'overlap_conf_threshold (default 0.7), overlap_surface_cap (default 3) on LLMConfig (src/rebar/llm/config.py, [tool.rebar.llm], REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_llm_float` helper mirroring `_llm_int` and call it in `from_env()`: `overlap_conf_threshold=_llm_float(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_CONF_THRESHOLD', 'overlap_conf_threshold', 0.7)` and `overlap_surface_cap=_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_SURFACE_CAP', 'overlap_surface_cap', 3)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the '4-way' vs. 5-enum discrepancy: either rename to '5-way classification' (duplicates / supersedes / depends_on / related_distinct / unrelated) or remove one value and update the schema to match.
findings.finding: The rubric prompt body requirement says 'MUST encode the 4-way classification' but the output schema defines 5 enum values (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated) — a conflicting signal that will confuse an executor writing the prompt.
findings.location: Scope section — 'rubric prompt … MUST encode: the 4-way classification' vs. 'What — output schema' — 'relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the 4-way classification' in the prompt-body requirement to 'the 5-way classification (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated)' to match the schema enum exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the '4-way classification' vs 5-enum contradiction: either update Scope to say '5-way (3 surfaceable + 2 non-surface)' or justify why only 4 classes appear in the prompt.
findings.finding: The Scope section instructs that the rubric prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification,' but the What section defines a 5-value relation enum ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}). '4-way' and '5-way' are contradictory; an implementer writing the prompt would not know which count is authoritative.
findings.location: Scope section ('the 4-way classification') vs What — output schema section ('relation ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}')
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope wording to: 'the 5-way classification (duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated — where only the first three are surfaceable)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 4-way vs 5-way classification contradiction: explicitly state whether `unrelated` is LLM-emittable (making it 5-way) or only an internal aggregation sentinel (making the prompt a true 4-way), and align the Scope instruction and the relation enum definition to match.
findings.finding: The Scope section mandates the prompt body 'MUST encode: the 4-way classification,' but the What section defines a 5-value `relation` enum ({duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}). An implementer writing overlap_judge.md is given contradictory cardinality: the prose says 4-way, the schema says 5-way. This is an undefined/misspecified vocabulary that the prompt author and the schema builder must both consume.
findings.location: Scope section ('the 4-way classification') vs. What section (relation enum with 5 values)
findings.suggested_fix: If `unrelated` is only a post-aggregation result (never emitted by the LLM), change the Scope instruction to '5-way classification' or document that the LLM sees only 4 choices (duplicates/supersedes/depends_on/related_distinct) and `unrelated` is reserved for the aggregation layer when abstain fires. Add a note to the schema: 'LLM-emittable values: {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct}; unrelated is reserved for aggregation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline the `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` fixture format spec in the Testing section (field names, ground-truth label vocabulary, file-per-pair vs single-file layout, and how label maps to the `relation` enum) so `test_judge_precision_recall` can be written unambiguously.
findings.finding: The Testing section states the `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` fixture format is 'documented' but provides no reference to where that documentation lives, and no such directory or format spec exists anywhere in the repo. Without a defined fixture schema (field names, label vocabulary, pair format), `test_judge_precision_recall` cannot be written consistently by different implementers.
findings.location: Testing section: 'LABELED fixture of ≥12 pairs (tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/, format documented)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: 'Each fixture file is a JSON object: `{"a": {"id": str, "digest": str}, "b": {"id": str, "digest": str}, "label": relation_enum_value, "notes": str|null}`. Label vocabulary matches the `relation` enum exactly; `related_distinct` and `unrelated` both count as negative (non-surfaced) ground truth for precision/recall computation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fixture format inline (e.g., a JSON array of `{id, doc_a, doc_b, label}` objects in `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/pairs.json`, where `label ∈ {duplicates, supersedes, depends_on, related_distinct, unrelated}`) so an executor can build the dataset without clarification.
findings.finding: The fixture format for `tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/` is described only as 'format documented' — the plan never specifies what that documentation contains (file extension, required fields, label vocabulary, one-file-per-pair vs. single manifest, etc.), leaving the executor unable to build the labeled dataset.
findings.location: Testing section — 'LABELED fixture of ≥12 pairs (tests/fixtures/overlap_pairs/, format documented)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: 'Fixture format: a single `pairs.json` file containing a JSON array of objects `{"id": string, "doc_a": {digest fields}, "doc_b": {digest fields}, "label": <enum value>}`. At least 12 entries required, with ≥2 positive examples per surfaceable relation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion (or extend an existing one) that explicitly verifies `overlap_verdict.schema.json` exists and `register_contract('overlap_verdict', ...)` is called in `contracts.py`.
findings.finding: The Scope describes two concrete deliverables — `src/rebar/schemas/overlap_verdict.schema.json` and the `register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)` call in `contracts.py` — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies their existence or correctness.
findings.location: Scope section ('overlap_verdict schema ... + register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)') vs Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC list: '- [ ] `src/rebar/schemas/overlap_verdict.schema.json` is present and `register_contract("overlap_verdict", <builder>)` appears in `contracts.py`; verified by a `test_contract_registered` unit test or a grep-based CI check.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the dual-ordering / position-bias aggregation rule, the restricted surfaceable-relation set, and the stage-2 judge's role in the pipeline (T7/T5e: novel architectural decision requires a doc/ADR home).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a non-trivial architectural decision — the dual-ordering aggregation rule with position-bias mitigation, the restriction of surfaceable relations to 3 of 6 built-ins, and the new pipeline stage sitting between S5 and S7 — but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capture is planned.
findings.location: ## What — output schema / ## Scope / ## Dependencies
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/0NNN-stage2-overlap-judge.md`) covering: (1) why dual-ordering, (2) the CLOSED surfaceable-relation set and its rationale, (3) the fail-safe downgrade policy, and (4) the stage's position between S5 and S7. Reference it from `CLAUDE.md` under the LLM pipeline section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an explicit upper bound for K (e.g., add `overlap_candidate_cap` to `LLMConfig` or note that S5 is responsible for capping it) and document the resulting worst-case LLM call count and estimated cost per query.
findings.finding: K (the number of stage-1 candidates fed to the judge) is never bounded by the plan. The plan asserts '2K judge calls, flat in corpus size' as a correctness claim, but if K is, say, 50 or 100, that is 100–200 LLM calls per query invocation with no stated cap, rate-limit, or concurrency model — making per-query cost and latency unbounded.
findings.location: What — output schema section: 'K candidates → 2K judge calls (both orderings), flat in corpus size.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `overlap_candidate_cap` (default e.g. 10) to `LLMConfig` alongside `overlap_conf_threshold`; the judge truncates the candidate list to this cap before issuing calls. Document in the plan: 'max 2 × overlap_candidate_cap LLM calls per query.' If S5 already enforces a hard cap, cross-reference it here explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reliability spec for the all-abstain case: when LLM error-rate across candidates exceeds a threshold (e.g. 100%), the judge MUST surface a structured warning/diagnostic to the caller (not just a log line), and specify at minimum one retry attempt with backoff before treating a call as abstain.
findings.finding: The plan specifies LLM error → immediate abstain (no retry/backoff). For transient failures (network blip, rate-limit 429, momentary LLM unavailability), every candidate pair will be silently dropped, producing 0 surfaced findings — indistinguishable from a corpus with no overlaps. No user-visible warning or degraded-mode indicator is specified for the case where all candidates abstain due to LLM errors.
findings.location: What — output schema: 'LLM timeout/error/malformed output on a candidate → treated as abstain (dropped), logged; never raises to the caller.' / Testing AC: `::test_llm_error_abstain`.
findings.suggested_fix: After all 2K calls complete, if error_count / (2K) >= some threshold (e.g. 0.5), return a diagnostic result flag (e.g., `judge_degraded: true`) alongside the empty surface list so S7/callers can distinguish degraded from clean. Add a single retry with exponential backoff (e.g., 1 attempt, 1s delay) before escalating to abstain for timeout/5xx errors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test `test_below_threshold_downgrade` (both orderings agree + non-null artifact, but at least one confidence < `overlap_conf_threshold`) asserting the result is NOT surfaced (downgraded to `related_distinct`).
findings.finding: No named test covers the case where both orderings agree on the same surfaceable relation but one or both confidence scores fall below `overlap_conf_threshold` (0.7) — a distinct aggregation path from disagreement that silently downgrades to `related_distinct` without surfacing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1; Testing section — named test list
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `tests/unit/test_overlap_judge.py`: `test_below_threshold_downgrade` — configure FakeRunner to return `{relation: 'duplicates', shared_artifact: 'X', confidence: 0.65, abstain: false}` for both orderings (below default 0.7 threshold) and assert zero surfaced results.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider deferring `overlap_conf_threshold` / `overlap_surface_cap` as `LLMConfig` fields (with `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars) until S7 creates a second call-site; for this story, pass them as constructor arguments to `JudgeConfig` or as plain kwargs to keep the LLMConfig surface clean until Rule-of-Three is met.
findings.finding: `overlap_conf_threshold` and `overlap_surface_cap` are proposed as new `LLMConfig` fields with dedicated `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_*` env vars and `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys, but each has exactly one consuming call-site in this story (`judge.py`), with S7 (the only downstream consumer) explicitly out of scope. This is below the Rule-of-Three threshold and constitutes config-surface proliferation: two new knobs are added to the shared `LLMConfig` data class and its env/file resolution machinery before the abstraction needs multi-site tuning.
findings.location: Config section: `overlap_conf_threshold` (default 0.7), `overlap_surface_cap` (default 3) on `LLMConfig`
findings.suggested_fix: Define a local `OverlapJudgeConfig` dataclass in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/judge.py` (or a sibling `config.py`) with `conf_threshold=0.7` and `surface_cap=3` as plain fields. Promote to `LLMConfig` + env vars in the S7 story, when the wiring layer provides the second call-site that satisfies Rule-of-Three.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Scope section explaining why `execution_mode: single_turn` is correct for the overlap judge (e.g. 'single_turn is appropriate because the judge is a pure classifier: it receives two digests and emits one structured verdict with no need for tool calls or multi-turn reasoning').
findings.finding: The plan specifies `execution_mode: single_turn` for the rubric prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` but provides no positive rationale for this choice. All other plan-review-criterion prompts (e.g. plan_review_G5.md, plan_review_E1.md) are single_turn, but the plan doesn't explain why single_turn is correct for the overlap judge rather than agentic (which could, for example, fetch additional ticket context via tools).
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md` (`execution_mode: single_turn`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion about LLM numeric confidence: either cite evidence that the chosen model emits calibrated probabilities for pairwise classification, or replace `confidence: number` with a discrete confidence tier (e.g., `high | medium | low`) mapped to a threshold table, and document the mapping in the rubric prompt body requirements.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `confidence: number` in the `overlap_verdict` schema and gates surfacing on `confidence >= overlap_conf_threshold` (0.7), implicitly asserting that an LLM can emit a reliably calibrated numeric probability for 'is this a duplicate?' — an unverified capability. LLMs are known to be poorly calibrated on self-reported numeric confidence, and the prompt design (rubric body requirements listed in Scope) contains no instruction to calibrate or explain the confidence score. The plan neither cites prior work on LLM confidence calibration in classification tasks nor specifies a discrete-tier fallback (e.g., high/medium/low → mapped to numeric range), leaving a real risk that the 0.7 threshold is meaningless in practice.
findings.location: What — output schema; Config (LLMConfig); Scope (rubric prompt body requirements)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `confidence: number` with `confidence_tier: 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'` in the schema; add a mapping table (e.g., high→1.0, medium→0.6, low→0.2) used by the aggregation rule instead of the raw numeric threshold. Update `overlap_conf_threshold` to accept a tier string or keep numeric but specify the prompt must instruct the model to use a defined rubric (e.g., 'high = you are >90% certain the shared_artifact is the same concrete thing').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the Scope section that adding overlap_judge.md to src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ makes it visible to _packaged_prompt_files() / build_prompt_index(), and confirm that index.json regeneration is NOT required (since category: overlap skips it from the reviewer index) — or relocate the prompt to a different directory (e.g. src/rebar/llm/overlap/) to keep it out of the reviewer scanner entirely.
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json is an unenumerated edit target. Placing overlap_judge.md in src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ causes _packaged_prompt_files() to scan it on every build_prompt_index() call. Although category: overlap means it is skipped from the reviewer index, the existing CI drift gate (test_index_on_disk_matches_regenerated) re-runs build_prompt_index() and rewrites index.json — if the implementor does not regenerate index.json after adding the file, that test will fail (or pass trivially only if the file is skipped with no side-effects). The plan does not mention regenerating index.json as a required step.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the rubric prompt to src/rebar/llm/overlap/overlap_judge.md (co-located with judge.py) to avoid any interaction with the reviewer prompt scanner and index.json drift gate. Update the Scope section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a prior-art justification for the dual-ordering position-bias protocol: cite the relevant literature or internal experiment, and address whether the 2K-call cost is justified vs. a single symmetric-prompt alternative; also clarify how the 'both orderings must agree' rule applies to inherently asymmetric relations like `supersedes`.
findings.finding: The plan's dual-ordering, both-A→B/B→A aggregation rule is a bespoke position-bias mitigation protocol, but the plan does not reference whether the well-established prior-art technique (e.g., the Chatbot Arena / MT-Bench dual-ordering calibration, or the simpler industry practice of averaging logprobs) was considered and rejected. The plan commits to this specific design with no mention of alternatives, making it impossible to evaluate whether the added complexity (2K calls instead of K) is the best trade-off or whether a single-call rubric with explicit symmetry instructions would suffice.
findings.location: What — output schema (Both orderings... AGGREGATION section); Why

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either register `category: overlap` in the reviewer category vocabulary (index.json and any category-validation logic), or use an existing category value (e.g. `plan-review-pass`) and document how overlap_judge.md is dispatched separately from the standard reviewer pipeline.
findings.finding: `category: overlap` is specified as the front-matter category for `overlap_judge.md`, but this value does not exist in any current reviewer category vocabulary. Exhaustive grep of all existing reviewer prompts shows categories: {plan-review-criterion, plan-review-pass, code-review-pass, review} — 'overlap' is absent. The category field is consumed by the reviewer loader/index; an unknown value may be silently ignored, cause a dispatch error, or prevent the prompt from being discoverable.
findings.location: Scope section: 'rubric prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/overlap_judge.md (execution_mode: single_turn, outputs: overlap_verdict, category: overlap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a comment block or `x-aggregation-rule` annotation to `overlap_verdict.schema.json` (or a companion `overlap_verdict_aggregation.md` note) that machine-pins the dual-ordering consensus gate conditions co-located with the schema, so implementers of judge.py have a single authoritative reference.
findings.finding: The dual-ordering aggregation decision rule ('surface ONLY if both orderings agree on the SAME surfaceable relation AND each cites a non-null shared_artifact AND both confidence ≥ overlap_conf_threshold; else downgrade to related_distinct') is specified only in the What/Output prose. It is not co-located with `overlap_verdict.schema.json` or with any machine-readable constraint on the schema — an implementer building judge.py must look to prose to find the rule that governs how two `overlap_verdict` instances combine into a surface decision.
findings.location: What section (aggregation rule prose) vs. Scope section (overlap_verdict.schema.json deliverable)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
